reference: GATE CS — Operating System / Processes and Threads

map.condition: Count child processes from n fork() calls in a loop
Compare mode-switch time vs process context-switch time
What threads share vs keep private
map.use: \text{Shared: code, data, heap, files} \quad \text{Private: stack, registers, PC, TID}

map.condition: What is saved on a context switch
map.use: \text{Saved: PC, registers, SP, PCB} \quad \text{Not necessarily: TLB}

formulas.name: Children created by n fork()s in a loop
formulas.latex: \text{For } i=1 \text{ to } n: \text{ fork()} \Rightarrow \text{total child processes} = 2^n - 1
formulas.plain: Loop: for(i=1; i<=n; i++) fork() => new (child) processes = 2^n - 1
formulas.whenToUse: When fork() is called n times inside a sequential loop (each existing process forks at each iteration).

terms.means: number of loop iterations (number of fork calls)
total number of newly created (child) processes
total number of processes including the original

formulas.trap: GATE often asks for 'new processes' (2^n - 1) vs 'total processes' (2^n). Don't confuse the two. Also, the loop variable i matters: for(i=0; i<n; i++) also gives 2^n - 1 children.

formulas.name: Total processes after n sequential forks
formulas.whenToUse: When the question asks for the total number of processes (parent + all descendants) after n fork() calls in a loop.

terms.means: total processes alive after n fork() calls in a loop

formulas.trap: If forks are placed outside a loop (sequential, not looped), the count differs. Each fork doubles the process count only when every existing process calls fork at each step.

formulas.name: fork() Return Value
formulas.latex: \text{fork()} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{in child process} \\ \text{child PID} > 0 & \text{in parent process} \\ -1 & \text{on error} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: fork() returns 0 in child, child-PID (>0) in parent, -1 on error
formulas.whenToUse: When reasoning about which branch (if/else) executes in the child vs parent after a fork() call.

terms.means: returned to the child process
returned to the parent (value = child's PID)
fork failed (no new process created)

formulas.trap: if(fork() == 0) runs in the child; the else branch runs in the parent. Both processes continue from the next line after fork() — they do NOT restart from main.

formulas.name: Address Space After fork()
formulas.latex: \text{Child gets a } \textit{copy} \text{ of parent's address space (COW)}
formulas.plain: Child gets an independent copy (Copy-On-Write) of parent's memory; same virtual addresses but different physical pages after write.
formulas.whenToUse: When a question asks about shared/separate variables or same memory address (&a) printing same value vs different value after fork().

terms.means: Copy-On-Write — physical copy made only on first write
virtual address (same in both parent and child, but different physical location)

formulas.trap: Same virtual address does NOT mean same physical memory after a write. Parent and child modify their own copies independently.

formulas.name: State Saved During Context Switch
formulas.latex: \text{PCB saves: PC, SP, registers, page table base, process state, open files}
formulas.plain: PCB (Process Control Block) saves: Program Counter, Stack Pointer, general-purpose registers, page table base register, process state, open file descriptors.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked what the OS saves/restores when switching from process A to process B.

terms.means: Process Control Block — data structure storing process context
Program Counter — next instruction address
Stack Pointer

formulas.trap: The TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) does NOT need to be saved — it is typically flushed or invalidated (or uses ASIDs) on a context switch, not saved to the PCB.

formulas.name: Mode Switch Time vs Context Switch Time
formulas.plain: T1 (user-to-kernel mode switch) < T2 (process-to-process context switch)
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing the overhead of switching CPU mode (user->kernel) vs switching between two user processes.

terms.means: time to switch from user mode to kernel mode (mode switch)
time to switch context between two processes (context switch)

formulas.trap: A mode switch only saves a few registers and changes the privilege level. A full context switch additionally saves the entire process state (PCB), swaps address spaces, may flush TLB — so T1 < T2.

formulas.name: TLB and Context Switch
formulas.plain: On context switch: TLB is flushed (invalidated) OR entries are tagged with Address Space Identifiers (ASIDs) to avoid full flush.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked which items need NOT be saved on a context switch (TLB is the classic answer).

terms.means: Translation Lookaside Buffer — hardware cache for page table entries
Address Space Identifier — tags TLB entries to avoid full flush

formulas.trap: TLB contents are NOT saved in the PCB. They are discarded (flushed) on switch. This is different from registers which ARE saved.

formulas.name: System Call Invocation Mechanism
formulas.plain: System calls are invoked via a software interrupt (trap instruction), which transfers control to the OS kernel.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how a user process invokes OS services (system calls).

terms.means: software-generated interrupt that switches CPU to kernel mode
privileged CPU mode where OS code executes

formulas.trap: System calls use a software interrupt (trap), NOT a hardware interrupt, NOT polling, NOT an indirect jump. The OS handles the trap and returns to user mode after service.

formulas.name: Switching from Privileged to Non-Privileged Mode
formulas.plain: Switching FROM kernel/privileged mode TO user/non-privileged mode requires a privileged instruction (executed by the OS, e.g., IRET on x86).
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how the CPU transitions from privileged (kernel) mode back to non-privileged (user) mode.

terms.means: interrupt return instruction (privileged) that restores user mode

formulas.trap: Going FROM privileged TO non-privileged requires a privileged instruction (not a software interrupt). A software interrupt goes FROM user TO kernel, not the reverse.

formulas.name: Dual-Mode Operation
formulas.plain: CPU has a mode bit: 0 = kernel mode, 1 = user mode. Privileged instructions only execute when mode bit = 0.
formulas.whenToUse: When reasoning about which instructions can execute in which mode, or what the mode bit indicates.

terms.means: 1-bit register in CPU indicating current privilege level
instruction that can only execute in kernel mode

formulas.trap: Not all instructions are privileged. I/O instructions, halt, interrupt control, and memory protection setup are privileged. Normal arithmetic/logic are available in both modes.

formulas.name: Signal Handling on Interrupt
formulas.plain: On Ctrl-C: kernel sends SIGINT, process runs user-registered signal handler (if any) in user mode, then terminates or resumes.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked what happens when a signal (SIGINT from Ctrl-C) is delivered to a process with a custom signal handler.

terms.means: signal sent on Ctrl-C input
user-defined function registered to handle a specific signal

formulas.trap: The signal handler runs in user mode, not kernel mode. The kernel only delivers the signal and sets up the handler invocation — the handler code itself is user-level.

formulas.name: Process State Transition Model
formulas.plain: New -> Ready -> (dispatch) -> Running -> (I/O wait) -> Blocked -> Ready; Running -> (preempt) -> Ready; Running -> (exit) -> Terminated
formulas.whenToUse: When asked to identify valid/invalid process state transitions or what causes a transition.

terms.means: scheduler selects process from Ready queue and gives CPU
OS removes CPU from running process (timer interrupt)
process waiting for I/O or an event to complete

formulas.trap: A process can go from Running -> Ready (preemption) and Running -> Blocked (I/O), but NOT directly from Blocked -> Running. It must go Blocked -> Ready -> Running.

formulas.name: What Interrupts a Running Process
formulas.latex: \text{Interrupts: Timer, I/O device, Power failure, Hardware fault} \quad \text{NOT: Scheduler process itself}
formulas.plain: Timer, I/O device completion, power failure, and hardware faults interrupt a running process. The scheduler (as a separate process) does NOT directly interrupt it — the timer interrupt invokes the scheduler.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked which events can interrupt or preempt a running process.

terms.means: hardware clock interrupt that triggers the OS scheduler
OS routine invoked by timer interrupt to decide next process

formulas.trap: The 'scheduler process' does NOT interrupt a running process on its own — the timer hardware interrupt does, which then causes the OS to run the scheduler. The scheduler is not an external interrupter.

formulas.name: Thread: Shared vs Private Resources
formulas.latex: \text{Shared (per process): code, data, heap, open files, signals} \quad \text{Private (per thread): stack, registers, PC, TID}
formulas.plain: Threads within a process share: code segment, data segment, heap, open file descriptors, signal handlers. Each thread has its own: stack, register set, program counter, thread ID.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked what is shared among threads of a process vs what each thread maintains privately.

terms.means: Thread ID — unique identifier for each thread
private per thread — stores local variables and function call frames
shared among all threads of a process

formulas.trap: Stack is PRIVATE per thread (not shared). Heap is shared. This is a frequent GATE trap — do not confuse stack with heap.

formulas.name: User-Level vs Kernel-Level Threads
formulas.latex: \text{ULT: managed in user space (thread library)} \quad \text{KLT: managed by OS kernel}
formulas.plain: User-Level Threads (ULT): managed by user-space library, kernel unaware, fast switching but blocks whole process on I/O. Kernel-Level Threads (KLT): kernel manages, true parallelism, slower creation/switch.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about thread management, blocking behavior, or parallelism in multi-core systems.

terms.means: User-Level Thread — kernel sees one process, thread library does scheduling
Kernel-Level Thread — kernel manages each thread, can schedule on separate CPUs

formulas.trap: With ULTs, if one thread makes a blocking system call, the entire process blocks (kernel doesn't know about other threads). KLTs avoid this but have higher overhead.

formulas.name: Thread Models (Many-to-One, One-to-One, Many-to-Many)
formulas.plain: Many-to-One: multiple user threads -> one kernel thread. One-to-One: each user thread -> one kernel thread. Many-to-Many: M user threads -> N kernel threads (M >= N).
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about threading models and their trade-offs (concurrency, blocking, parallelism).

terms.means: Many user threads to one kernel thread — no true parallelism
Each user thread has a kernel thread — true parallelism, higher overhead
M user threads multiplexed onto N kernel threads — balanced

formulas.trap: M:1 model cannot use multiple CPU cores simultaneously. 1:1 model has overhead from kernel thread creation for each user thread.

formulas.name: Thread vs Process Creation Overhead
formulas.latex: T_{\text{thread creation}} \ll T_{\text{process creation}} \quad (\text{no address space copy for threads})
formulas.plain: Thread creation is much faster than process creation because threads share the process address space (no copy needed). Context switch between threads is also faster than between processes.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked why threads are called 'lightweight processes' or comparing thread vs process overhead.

terms.means: thread — shares address space, faster to create and switch

formulas.trap: Threads are NOT independent — a bug in one thread (e.g., corrupting heap) can crash all threads in the same process. Processes are better isolated.

formulas.name: Thread Synchronization Requirement
formulas.plain: When multiple threads access shared data concurrently with at least one write, a race condition can occur. Synchronization (mutex, semaphore) is required to ensure correctness.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked why threads need synchronization primitives or about race conditions in multithreaded programs.

terms.means: outcome depends on interleaving order of thread execution
mutual exclusion lock to protect critical sections

formulas.trap: Read-only shared access by multiple threads is safe without synchronization. Race conditions only arise when at least one thread writes to shared data.

formulas.name: Process Memory Segments
formulas.plain: Process memory: Text (code, read-only) | Initialized Data | BSS (uninitialized data) | Heap (grows up) | ... | Stack (grows down)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about process address space layout or where variables (global, local, dynamic) are stored.

terms.means: code segment — read-only, shared among threads
dynamic allocation (malloc/new), shared by all threads, grows upward
local variables, function frames, private per thread, grows downward

formulas.trap: Global/static variables are in the Data/BSS segment, NOT the heap. Local variables are on the stack. Dynamically allocated memory is on the heap.
